Stats — The Free Open-Source macOS Menu Bar System Monitor I Use Every Day (CPU, GPU, Temperature, Network)

A walkthrough of Stats — the open-source macOS menu bar system monitor I run on every Mac I own. Covers what it shows (CPU, GPU, RAM, disk, network, battery, sensor temperatures, Bluetooth, multi-timezone clock, plus a Remote module), how to install it with `brew install --cask stats`, a quick tour through each module, the desktop widget trick, and a couple of macOS 26 widget quirks worth knowing about.
Why CSS overflow: scroll Shows No Scrollbar on macOS Chrome (vs. Windows)

You set overflow: scroll, then realize Mac Chrome shows no scrollbar — but your coworker on Windows sees one fine. This post starts with the four overflow values, then explains macOS's Overlay Scrollbar mechanism — why Chrome on macOS doesn't always show a scrollbar even with overflow: scroll. Three fixes: ::-webkit-scrollbar to force it, the newer standard scrollbar-width and scrollbar-color, and flipping macOS's "Show scroll bars" system setting to "Always".
How Should You Install Software on a Mac? Homebrew vs Official Download vs curl

If you've just started using a Mac for development, you've probably noticed there are several different ways to install the same tool — download a `.dmg` from the official site, install with Homebrew, run a `curl` install script, and tutorials sometimes recommend different approaches for the same tool. This post sorts through the common Mac install methods, pairs them with real examples (Git, Node.js, Python, VS Code), so you know which one to pick when.
macOS Time Machine Taking Forever? One Trick to Speed It Up

macOS Time Machine is a great backup tool, but the backup process sometimes gets painfully slow — especially if you haven't run it for a while. This post walks through the fastest way to get Time Machine going again, so backing up stops being a chore.
Creating a Windows Install USB From macOS
Need to reinstall Windows but only have a Mac? Making a Windows 10 install USB on macOS — ExFAT vs MS-DOS, where to grab the ISO, and writing the image.
Expose a Local Server to the Internet With ngrok (Windows + macOS Setup)

ngrok is a handy way to expose a local dev server to the internet — roughly 5 minutes of setup gets you a public HTTPS URL that forwards to your localhost. The first-run setup has a few quirks (especially on macOS — Gatekeeper blocks the binary; PATH needs configuring), so this post walks through Windows + macOS setup end to end. Useful for sharing a local prototype, testing webhooks, or wiring up a Line Bot without deploying.
What the Hell Is .DS_Store? Should You Commit It to Git?

What actually is .DS_Store? Should it go in your Git repo? Why does it even exist? This post walks through what .DS_Store is, why every macOS folder has one, and why you should keep it out of version control.
The Complete Mac Terminal Setup: iTerm2 + Zsh + Oh My Zsh + Powerlevel10k

A complete walkthrough of how I customize my Mac terminal — installing Homebrew, iTerm2, Zsh, Oh My Zsh, useful Zsh plugins (zsh-completions, zsh-autosuggestions, zsh-syntax-highlighting), themes (agnoster, Powerlevel10k), iTerm2 color schemes, and the edge cases that bite you (nvm, Homebrew PATH, VSCode font). Plus my full .zshrc for reference.
